cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure this out. The question is about why a novelist can expect the publisher to print their manuscript exactly as written. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I'll need to refer back to the passage provided. The passage starts by talking about the novelist's medium being the written word and how they have control over their text. It mentions that the novelist keeps absolute control until it's published. It also says that even if the writer doesn't want to revise based on the editor's advice, people wouldn't be surprised. Established novelists might deliver their manuscript expecting it to be printed as is. Looking at the options: A) Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text. The passage does say that the novelist keeps absolute control until it's published. So maybe this is the reason. B) The paperback being portable and adaptable is a point made, but how does that relate to the publisher printing it as is? Maybe the portability doesn't affect the control the novelist has. C) The novel is limited to a single channel of information. The passage says that the novel is limited to writing, which allows it to go anywhere without practical concerns. But how does that directly lead to the expectation of it being printed as written? It might contribute to the writer's control because they don't have to consider other elements like staging or film production. D) The novelist is seldom advised by editors. But the passage says that the writer may be advised to revise but can refuse. So the fact that they are advised doesn't mean they have to. The answer might not be D because it's not about being seldom advised, but about having the right to refuse. So the passage directly states that the novelist keeps absolute control over the text until publication. So when the question is why they can expect it to be printed as written, the answer is because they have that absolute control. So option A.
